coredata.dc:title: A new fluorescent nitrogen-doped carbon dot system modified by the fluorophore-labeled ssDNA for the analysis of 6-mercaptopurine and Hg (II)
coredata.prism:copyright: Copyright © 2015 Elsevier B.V. All rights reserved.
coredata.dc:creator: Li, Zhuo
Ni, Yongnian
Kokot, Serge
coredata.dc:description: A simple, environmentally friendly hydrothermal method was used to prepare strongly luminescent, nitrogen-doped carbon dots (NCDs) with the use of Chinese yams as a source of carbon and nitrogen. Such NCDs have an average size of 2.7±1.4nm; they emit blue light at 420nm and have a quantum yield of up to 9.3%. Thus, carboxyfluorescein (FAM)-DNA macro-molecules were assembled on the surfaces of the NCDs, and stabilised by strong π–π stacking; the so formed hybrid nano-sensors were found to have an ultra-sensitive response to 6-mercaptopurine (6-MP). A strong emission and enhancement of yellow radiation was observed from FAM. Furthermore, due to the specific interactions between DNA and Hg2+, which resulted in the formation of the T–Hg2+–T (T: thymine base) complex – a large, conjugated system, which formed between NCDs, DNA and 6-MP, was broken up. Thus, the fluorescence from FAM was quenched. The detection limits for 6-MP and Hg2+ were 0.67 and 1.26nM, respectively. The proposed method was applied for the determination of 6-MP in human serum and Hg2+ in water samples with satisfactory results.
coredata.dcterms:subject: Carbon dots
FAM-DNA
6-mercaptopurine
Mercury ions
Fluorescence switching
Chinese yam

xocs:item-toc-entry.xocs:item-toc-section-title: Performance of the novel method for the analysis of 6-MP and Hg2+
Selectivity of the novel sensor for 6-MP and Hg2+ analysis
Performance of the novel sensor for the analysis of 6-MP and Hg2+ in human serum and water samples

xocs:funding.xocs:funding-agency-matched-string: State Key Laboratory of Food Science and Technology
xocs:funding.xocs:funding-agency: State Key Laboratory of Food Science and Technology

xocs:funding-list.xocs:funding-text: The authors acknowledge the financial support by the Graduate Student Innovation Program ( YC2014-S042 ) and the State Key Laboratory of Food Science and Technology ( SKLF-ZZA201302 ) of Nanchang University . Appendix A

ce:caption.ce:simple-para: (A) Contour fluorescence spectra of NCDs; (B) excitation and emission spectra of NCDs, inset: photos of NCD solutions under visible light and UV radiation at 365nm, respectively; (C) fluorescence spectra of NCDs excited at different wavelengths, inset: corresponding CIE (x, y) chromaticity diagram; (D) FT-IR spectrum of NCDs; (E) XPS survey spectrum of NCDs and the enlarged regions for (F) C1s, (G) N1s, and (H) O1s.

(A) Fluorescence emission spectra of: (a) NCDs, (b) NCDs–ssDNA, (c) NCDs–ssDNA+1.0μM 6-MP, and (d) NCDs–ssDNA+1.0μM 6-MP+25U Dnase; (B) fluorescence emission spectra of: (a) NCDs, (b) NCDs–ssDNA, (c) NCDs–ssDNA+1.0μM 6-MP, (d) NCDs–ssDNA+1.0μM 6-MP+1.0μM Hg2+, (e) NCDs–ssDNA+1.0μM 6-MP+2μM Hg2+, and (f) NCDs–ssDNA+1.0μM 6-MP+3.0μM Hg2+; (C) UV spectra of: NCDs, NCDs–ssDNA, NCDs–ssDNA+6-MP, NCDs–ssDNA+6-MP+Hg2+. Inset: photos of (a) NCDs–ssDNA, (b) NCDs–ssDNA+1.0μM 6-MP, (c) NCDs–ssDNA+10μM 6-MP, (d) NCDs–ssDNA+10μM 6-MP+1.0μM Hg2+, and (e) NCDs–ssDNA+10μM 6-MP+10μM Hg2+ exposed to a 365nm UV lamp; (D) time-resolved fluorescence decay curves of (a) NCDs, (b) NCDs–ssDNA, (c) NCDs–ssDNA+6MP, and (d) NCDs–ssDNA+6-MP+Hg2+; (E) CD spectra of ssDNA, with different concentrations of 6-MP; (F) CD spectra of ssDNA, with different concentrations of Hg2+ in the presence of 6MP.

(A) Fluorescence emission spectra of NCDs–ssDNA with different concentrations of 6-MP (0, 2, 4, 136nM), inset 1: calibration plot: (F 1−F 0)/F 0 versus 6-MP (0–136nM), inset 2: plot of (F 1−F 0)/F 0 versus 6-MP (0–64nM); (B) fluorescence emission spectra of NCDs–ssDNA in the presence of 6-MP (80nM) in Hg2+ of different concentration (0, 5, 10, …, 170nM), inset 1: calibration plot (F 1−F 0)/F 0 versus Hg2+ (0–170nM), inset 2: plot of (F 1−F 0)/F 0 versus Hg2+ (0–65nM); (C) bar chart of the responses of NCDs–ssDNA to 6-MP (normalized), and other possibly present compounds; (D) bar chart of the responses (normalized) of NCDs–ssDNA in presence of 6-MP (80nM) to Hg2+ and other metal ions.
Analysis of 6-MP and Hg2+: performance comparison of the novel method with other methods.

ce:table-footnote.ce:label: b
ce:table-footnote.ce:note-para: Nano-composite of Au nano-clusters and CdTe quantum dots.

ce:table-footnote.ce:label: c
ce:table-footnote.ce:note-para: CDs were prepared by pyrolysis of ethylenediaminetetraacetic acid (EDTA) salts.

ce:caption.ce:simple-para: Recoveries of 6-MP and Hg2+ in natural samples (n=3).

entry.italic: Analysis of 6-MP in human serum albumin

head.ce:title: A new fluorescent nitrogen-doped carbon dot system modified by the fluorophore-labeled ssDNA for the analysis of 6-mercaptopurine and Hg (II)

ce:affiliation.ce:label: a
ce:affiliation.ce:textfn: College of Chemistry, Nanchang University, Nanchang 330031, China

ce:affiliation.ce:label: b
ce:affiliation.ce:textfn: State Key Laboratory of Food Science and Technology, Nanchang University, Nanchang 330047, China

sa:affiliation.sa:organization: State Key Laboratory of Food Science and Technology, Nanchang University

ce:affiliation.ce:label: c
ce:affiliation.ce:textfn: School of Chemistry, Physics and Mechanical Engineering, Science and Engineering Faculty, Queensland University of Technology, Brisbane 4001, Australia

sa:affiliation.sa:organization: School of Chemistry, Physics and Mechanical Engineering, Science and Engineering Faculty, Queensland University of Technology

ce:correspondence.ce:text: Corresponding author at: College of Chemistry, Nanchang University, Nanchang 330031, China. Fax: +86 79183969500.

ce:abstract-sec.ce:simple-para: A simple, environmentally friendly hydrothermal method was used to prepare strongly luminescent, nitrogen-doped carbon dots (NCDs) with the use of Chinese yams as a source of carbon and nitrogen. Such NCDs have an average size of 2.7±1.4nm; they emit blue light at 420nm and have a quantum yield of up to 9.3%. Thus, carboxyfluorescein (FAM)-DNA macro-molecules were assembled on the surfaces of the NCDs, and stabilised by strong π–π stacking; the so formed hybrid nano-sensors were found to have an ultra-sensitive response to 6-mercaptopurine (6-MP). A strong emission and enhancement of yellow radiation was observed from FAM. Furthermore, due to the specific interactions between DNA and Hg2+, which resulted in the formation of the T–Hg2+–T (T: thymine base) complex – a large, conjugated system, which formed between NCDs, DNA and 6-MP, was broken up. Thus, the fluorescence from FAM was quenched. The detection limits for 6-MP and Hg2+ were 0.67 and 1.26nM, respectively. The proposed method was applied for the determination of 6-MP in human serum and Hg2+ in water samples with satisfactory results.

ce:section.ce:para: Carbon nanomaterials are of current interest, because they have useful optical and electrochemical properties; also, they are generally biocompatible and have low toxicity (Guo and Dong, 2011; Novoselov et al., 2004). As a member of the nano-carbon family, carbon dots (CDs), which are less than 10nm in size, pose less environmental hazards and have better water solubility, chemical inertness as well as a high resistance to photo-bleaching in comparison to semiconductor quantum dots (Wang and Hu, 2014). Thus, the superior low toxicity and biocompatibility of the CDs facilitate potential applications in bio-imaging, -sensing and -molecule/drug delivery (Jia et al., 2012; Yang et al., 2009). In general, some recent studies on the synthesis of the CDs suggested that there are two types of method, “top-down” and “bottom-up” (Wang and Hu, 2014; Zhu et al., 2013). The former includes: electrochemical oxidation (Zhao et al., 2008; Zhou et al., 2007; Lu et al., 2009), acidic oxidation (Dong et al., 2010), laser ablation (Hu et al., 2009) and arc discharge (Xu et al., 2004) of mass carbon materials, such as graphite and carbon nanotubes amongst others; these materials involve hydrothermal (Zhu et al., 2012), ultrasonic (Zhuo et al., 2012) and microwave (Salinas-Castillo et al., 2013) processing of various organic precursors, such as glycerol (Lai et al., 2012), coffee grounds (Hsu et al., 2012), soy milk (Zhu et al., 2012), grass (Liu et al., 2012), eggs (Wang et al., 2012), gelatin (Liang et al., 2013), pomelo peel (Lu et al., 2012), honey (Yang et al., 2014) among others. Also, carbon materials containing heteroatoms, such as boron (Fan et al., 2014), nitrogen (Zhang and Chen, 2014) and phosphorus (Zhou et al., 2014), have been investigated and they are promising candidates, which complement carbon in materials.

In this work, a strategy was developed for the preparation of water-soluble, fluorescent N-doped carbon dots (NCDs; quantum yield ~9.3%) with the use of a hydrothermal process, which included the Chinese yam (Dioscorea spp.) as the carbon source.

Chinese yam is a tubular root, which is a common food and is also a traditional Chinese medicine used for the treatment of a variety of ailments, e.g. asthma, diabetes and diarrhea among others (Ju et al., 2014). It contains many chemical components, e.g. amino acids, alkaloid, glucoprotein, and polysaccharides (Yang et al., 2003), which serve as abundant sources of carbon and nitrogen for the preparation of NCDs.

Mercury is a heavy transition-metal, which is highly toxic and is quite abundant in the environment, particularly as water soluble ions (Hg2+) (Kim et al., 2012). Mercury, usually in its ionic form, can accumulate in humans generally from contaminated water and food, particularly sea food. It is toxic even at very low concentrations and can damage many human organs including brain, kidney, and the nervous system (Nolan and Lippard, 2008). There are many well-known methods for analysis of Hg2+, e.g. atomic absorption spectrometry (AAS) (Gil et al., 2006), inductively coupled plasma atomic emission spectrometry (ICP-AES) (Zhu et al., 2008), chemiluminescence (Li et al., 2011), electrochemical analysis (EC) (Lin et al., 2011) and surface-enhanced Raman scattering (SERS) (Du et al., 2013).

6-Mercaptopurine (6-MP) is an anti-cancer drug, which uses its toxic properties to suppress cancers such as acute lymphoblastic leukemia and inflammatory bowel diseases (Sahasranaman et al., 2008; Nielsen et al., 2001). The concentration of 6-MP in the plasma of a recipient varies, and it depends on individual differences (Zimm et al., 1983), which affect the activity of the anti-cancer drug. Consequently, it is important to monitor the concentrations of 6-MP in human serum. Several methods, such as traditional fluorescence (FL) (Wang and Zhang, 2008), chemiluminescence (CL) (Yang et al., 2008), electrochemical (EC) (Sato et al., 2005; Lu et al., 2008), high performance liquid chromatography (HPLC) (Su et al., 1999), and fluorescence analysis based on quantum dots (Chen et al., 2013; Gao et al., 2013) have been used to analyze 6-MP.

Generally, the common methods mentioned above for the detection of Hg2+ and 6-MP have some limitations, including time-consuming pretreatments, complex processes, and sometimes, relatively low sensitivity and poor selectivity. Thus, it would be useful to have an alternative but quite simple and relatively inexpensive, novel method for quantitative analysis of Hg2+ and 6-MP. Consequently, the aims of this work were: 1. to research and develop a new method for the preparation for CDs, and use this new method to synthesize a novel fluorescent nano-composite, which incorporates FAM, a fluorophore label, attached to a single-strand DNA (ssDNA); 2. to apply this nano-sensor for analysis of 6-MP and Hg2+ and compare the results with the existing methods; and 3. to research and elucidate the detection mechanism involved in the analysis of the above mentioned analytes.

Fresh Chinese yams were purchased from a local supermarket in Nanchang city. Mercury perchlorate trihydrate (HgCl2O8·3H2O), hydrogen peroxide, and 6-mercaptopurine (6-MP) were purchased from Sinopharm Chemical Reagent Co., Shanghai, China. Deoxyribonuclease (DNase, for short) was purchased from Sigma-Aldrich, St. Louis, MO, U.S. Adenine(A), cytosine(C), guanine(G), thymine(T), uracil(U), calf-thymus DNA and chemically synthesized oligonucleotide, i.e. ssDNA, used in this work, were purchased from Sangon Biotech Co., Shanghai, China. The oligonucleotide sequence was listed as: 5′-FAM-CTT GTT GTC CTG TTG TTC-3′.

All chemicals used were Analytical Grade reagents. Glassware was cleaned sequentially with chromic acid solution, aqua regia and double-distilled water (three times). Britton–Robinson (B–R) buffers with different pH values, were prepared by making a mixture of acids (2.71mL 85% H3PO4+2.36mL CH3COOH+2.47g H3BO3) L−1, and adjusting the pH with 0.2molL−1 NaOH in different proportions.
In this study, several techniques were used to characterize the CDs, and the descriptions of the instruments as well as the conditions of use can be found in Supplementary material.
Two steps were used to prepare NCDs–ssDNA according to Chen's method (Chen et al., 2015), and the detailed procedure is available in Supplementary material.

TEM was used to obtain information on the size and structure of NCDs–ssDNA, which was synthesized at different reaction times – 80min (Fig. S1A and B), 100min (Fig. S1C), and 120min (Fig. S1D and E); the results showed that the size distribution of the NCDs was mostly (82%) in the 1.5–4.0nm range (Inset 1, Fig. S1E). AFM (Fig. S1G and H) and XRD (Fig. S1I) were also used to characterize the NCDs.

Spectroscopic techniques were used to characterize the as-prepared NCDs, such as fluorescence ( Fig. 1A–C) and FT-IR (Fig. 1D). The composition of the NCDs was also characterized by XPS, and it was found that the NCDs mainly contained carbon, oxygen and nitrogen elements (Fig. 1E–H). All the details for characterization processes are recorded in Supplementary material.

There have been a number of recent reports concerned with the use of DNA in combination with carbonaceous materials for the analysis of Hg2+ (Cui et al., 2015; Wei et al., 2014a; Gao et al., 2014). These methods usually involved the labeling of the ssDNA with a fluorophore, e.g. FAM, ROX or nano-quantum dots, e.g. carbon or grapheme quantum dots; such labeled DNA molecules emit fluorescence when appropriately excited. In such processes, when a carbonaceous material, which has a large π–π conjugation platform, e.g. graphene oxide or carbon nanotubes, was added, the ssDNA was adsorbed onto the surface of the carbonaceous material as a result of π–π stacking. In these cases, fluorescence was quenched because fluorescence resonance energy transfer occurred. On addition of the Hg2+ analyte, hairpin structures formed following the formation of T–Hg2+–T complexes, and as a result, this assisted the ssDNA to break away from the surface of the platform. Consequently, the fluorescence intensity increased. This approach was also used for the detection of other heavy metal ions (e.g. Ag+, Pb2+) (Wei et al., 2014b; Liu et al., 2014) or target DNA (complementary strand) (Zhang et al., 2014). Any variation of the fluorescence signal noted in these reference studies was based on the “On–Off–On” mechanism.

The strategy of the method described in this work is quite different, i.e. the variation of the fluorescence signal was based not only on the “Off–On–Off” mechanism, but rather on the strategy, which included the NCDs–ssDNA in the sensing of 6-MP and Hg2+. NCDs can strongly bind FAM-DNA via π–π stacking between the nucleobases and C=O/C=N groups. Since the concentration of FAM-DNA is low, most FAM-DNA would be adsorbed on the surface of the NCDs in stirred solution. It is well known that the optimal λ ex/λ em for FAM is about 495/520nm (Table for fluorochromes, http://www.biotech.iastate.edu/facilities/flow/Fluorochromes.pdf). However, only the blue emission of the NCDs at 420nm could be observed (curves a and b, Fig. 2A and B). This occurred because FAM was present at low concentration and the excitation wavelength (320nm) was not optimal.

6-MP can combine with NCDs and FAM-DNA by forming chemical bonds, hydrogen bonds of base−pairs, and π–π conjugations. Thus, a large, conjugated system consisting of 6MP, NCDs, and FAM-DNA was formed and it was associated with a fluorescence peak at 525nm FAM; this peak was significantly enhanced (curve c, Fig. 2A and B). However, when Hg2+ was added, it combined specifically with the DNA by forming T–Hg2+–T complexes, and the related hairpin structures would favor the breakaway of the FAM-DNA from the NCDs. Thus, the big conjugation system was broken up, and the fluorescence intensity at 525nm was considerably quenched (curves d–f, Fig. 2B).

The corresponding spectral changes during the enhancing and quenching processes are displayed in Fig. 2A and B. Thus, the fluorescence peak from FAM occurred at 525nm, and the fluorescence was significantly enhanced in the presence of the added 6-MP (curve c). In addition, when a nuclease, DNase, was added and the mixture was allowed to stand for 30min, the fluorescence intensity decreased considerably (curve d, Fig. 2A); this occurred because the large conjugated system formed from the NCDs, FAM-DNA and 6-MP, was broken up because of the enzymatic hydrolysis of FAM-DNA. This phenomenon further confirmed that the formation or break up of a large conjugation system would significantly enhance or quench the fluorescence of FAM, respectively.

The UV–vis spectra of NCDs, NCDs–ssDNA, NCDs–ssDNA+6-MP (Fig. 2C) would assist in the rationalization of the NCDs–ssDNA+6-MP+Hg2+ interactions, and further help to elucidate the reaction mechanism. Thus, with the addition of 6-MP, a new peak at 495nm formed, which when compared with the spectrum of NCDs–ssDNA, demonstrated that the large conjugation system contributed to the yellow emission from FAM. On the other hand, when Hg2+ was added to the reaction mixture, the peak at 495nm disappeared. The insets (Fig. 2C) display photographs of: (a) NCDs–ssDNA, (b) NCDs–ssDNA+1.0μM 6-MP, (c) NCDs–ssDNA+10μM 6-MP, (d) NCDs–ssDNA+10μM 6-MP+1.0μM Hg2+, and (e) NCDs–ssDNA+10μM 6-MP+10μM Hg2+. All were taken when variously substituted DNA molecules were treated under a UV lamp at 365nm. These samples displayed a process during which the yellow emission of FAM became enhanced and gradually quenched. Time-resolved fluorescence decay curves (Fig. 2D) of the NCDs, NCDs–ssDNA, NCDs–ssDNA+6-MP, and NCDs–ssDNA+6-MP+Hg2+ systems were also obtained. Such plots were well fitted with the use of the bi-exponential functions, which were characterized by a short τ1 and a long τ2 lifetime components. The plots indicated that τ2 varied from 1.53 to 3.29ns and then 1.98ns, while τ1 remained almost unchanged (6.32ns). A recent report (Kim et al., 2013) suggested that the fast component referred to above arose from a band-to-band transition, whereas the slow component was associated with the edges of the NCDs–ssDNA combination; the changing τ2 confirmed that 6-MP may possibly be bound at the edges of the NCDs–ssDNA combination. On the other hand, the negligible changes of τ1 values demonstrated that the quenching effect of Hg2+ was not stopped by its adsorption on the surface of the NCDs. These observations further confirmed that Hg2+ ions combine with the ssDNA by forming the T–Hg2+–T hairpin structures, and this leads to the breakaway of FAM-DNA from the NCDs.

The CD spectrum of the free FAM-DNA (Fig. 2E) has a minimum at about 258nm and a maximum at 284nm; these bands are considered to be highly sensitive to the interactions of small molecules with DNA (Maheswari and Palaniandavar, 2004). In general, it is considered that the binding of a molecule with DNA may either stabilize or destabilize the DNA structure. Thus, the secondary structure of the DNA can be altered by intercalation with small molecules (Jain et al., 2003).

In the case of the double-strand DNA, minor groove binding with 6-MP does not influence significantly the CD spectrum of the DNA. In order to obtain further information about the binding of 6-MP to ssDNA, the CD spectra of ssDNA were recorded as the concentration of 6-MP (0.5–2.5μM) increased, and there were no noticeable changes in the CD spectra. This confirmed that the presence of 6-MP will not lead to changes in the conformation of the ssDNA. Similarly, the CD spectra of ssDNA at different concentrations of Hg2+ (0.5–3.5μM) in the presence of 6-MP were obtained (Fig. 2F), and it was observed that the emission intensity at 258nm gradually became stronger; this peak red shifted to 275nm. On the other hand, the positive peak at 284nm became weaker and red shifted to 296nm. However, the band from the 1.5μM sample (light blue, Fig. 2F) began to decrease in intensity at about 275nm and reached a minimum with the 3.5μM sample. However, a relatively intense broad band developed with the 2.0μM sample, and it had a maximum at 300nm. These results indicated that Hg2+ induced significant changes to the ssDNA conformation.
To improve the performance of the proposed sensor, experimental conditions such as the concentration of FAM-DNA, reaction time and pH, were optimized (see Supplementary material).

ce:section.ce:section-title: Performance of the novel method for the analysis of 6-MP and Hg2+
ce:section.ce:para: As previously noted (Section 3.2), the yellow emission peak of FAM in NCDs–ssDNA occurred with the addition of 6-MP due to both energy resonance transfer and the formation of large conjugated, rigid, plane structures. In practice, NCDs–ssDNA (20μL) was added to 1.97mL B–R buffer (pH=6.0) in each of the 22 tubes, and then a 10μL aliquot of 6-MP (0, 2, 4, 136nM; total: 22 samples) was added to each of these tubes. The enhanced fluorescence spectrum of each solution was recorded immediately at λ ex=320nm and λ em=525nm, and a calibration plot for 6-MP was established ( Fig. 3A). It was found that the intensity at 525nm (F 1) increased linearly with the concentration of 6-MP. The quenching ratio (F 1−F 0)/F 0 was linear with the concentration of 6-MP in the range of 1.0–64nmolL−1 (inset 2: LOD=0.67nmolL−1, S/N=3, r=99.75%). A performance comparison of the novel method for the analysis of 6MP from this work with that from some other studies ( Table 1), demonstrated that the proposed novel method has high sensitivity, a useful linear response range and a comparable LOD to that found in other studies.

During the reaction process, the fluorescence of the FAM in NCDs–ssDNA was quenched by the added Hg2+ (Fig. 3B), because the T–Hg2+–T complex was formed, and the large conjugation system, formed from the NCDs–ssDNA and the 6-MP, was broken up. Here, Hg2+ solutions (10μL each) of different concentration (final: 0, 5, 10, 15, 170nM, total 36 samples) were then added to the 6-MP solution (80nM in 1.97mL B–R buffer with 20μL NCDs–ssDNA). The fluorescence intensity (F 1) at 525nm decreased with the increasing concentration of Hg2+. The ratio, (F 1−F 0)/F 0 versus concentration of Hg2+ was linear over the 1.0–65nmolL−1 range (inset 2: LOD=1.26nmolL−1, S/N=3, r=99.92%).

The recommended maximum concentration level of Hg2+ in drinking water is 0.002mgL−1 (i.e. 9.97nmolL−1) (http://www.epa.gov/safewater/dwh/c-ioc/mercury.html). Thus, the conditions of the trial analytical model described above are consistent with the concentration level suggested by EPA (US Environmental Protection Agency). The performance of the proposed method was also compared with others for the analysis of Hg2+ (Chai et al., 2010; Paramanik et al., 2013; Zhou et al., 2012; Xu et al., 2011), and it was found that both the obtained high sensitivity and linearity compared well with the other methods (Table 1). Thus, a novel sensor for quantitative analysis of 6-MP and Hg2+ has been successfully constructed.

ce:section.ce:section-title: Selectivity of the novel sensor for 6-MP and Hg2+ analysis
ce:section.ce:para: Method selectivity was investigated by analyzing the response of the NCDs–ssDNA to 6-MP in the presence of some structurally similar compounds, which are commonly found in vivo. These substances included: BSA, HSA, calf-thymus DNA, VB2, VB6, VC, glucose, dopamine, cysteine, histidine, glutathione, adenine (A), guanine(G), cytosine(C), thymine(T), and uracil(U). The concentration of each was 50nM, which was the same as that of the 6-MP in solutions. In each case, no significant change was noted in comparison to the 6-MP analytical result, i.e. the error in the relative fluorescence intensity at 525nm was less than 5% in all cases as shown in Fig. 3C.

The emitted fluorescence of the FAM in NCDs–ssDNA may also be quenched by metal ions, and such quenching may affect this sensor for Hg2+. Thus, the following metal ions (each 50nmolL−1, i.e. at the same concentration as the Hg2+ in the test solutions) were analyzed: Ag+, Au3+, Pb2+, Cu2+, Al3+, Fe3+, Ce3+, Ba2+, Ca2+, Cd2+, Co2+, Mg2+, Mn2+, Ni2+, Sn4+, Zn2+, Cr3+, and K+ (Fig. 3D). The results showed that Ag+, Au3+, Pb2+, and Cu2+ had relatively large quenching effects on the fluorescence of FAM in NCDs–ssDNA. It is possible that these ions could also easily combine with DNA, and thus, be able to break up the large molecular conjugation system. The other 14 cations were investigated but they all showed a very low quenching effect – no higher than 4%. This result suggested that the novel sensor for Hg2+ has high selectivity for a wide range of cations.

ce:section.ce:section-title: Performance of the novel sensor for the analysis of 6-MP and Hg2+ in human serum and water samples
ce:section.ce:para: The proposed method was applied for the determination of 6-MP in human serum samples, which were obtained by dissolving 200mg of the serum powder in 5mL double-distilled water; then, the sample was spiked with 6-MP at three different concentrations, 10.0, 20.0, and 30.0nmolL−1. Following this procedure, saturated ammonium sulfate solution (2.0mL) was added to neutralize any remaining dissociated globulin. The results ( Table 2) were quite acceptable: % Recovery ranged from 92.2% to 106.5%, and the relative standard deviation (RSD) was less than 9%.

Also, standard Hg2+ samples with different concentrations were added to water samples (final concentrations of Hg2+ were 10.0, 20.0, and 30.0nmolL−1), which were collected from a local lake in Nanchang city. The results (Table 2) showed that the % Recovery ranged from 95.4% to 108.3%, and the RSD was less than 7%. These results suggested that the analysis of 6-MP and Hg2+ in water samples was satisfactory.

A new, environmentally friendly, hydrothermal method was developed for the preparation of luminescent NCDs using the Chinese yam as the precursor. Then, the NCDs were modified by their deposition on the surface of FAM-DNA, which facilitated strong π–π stacking. This hybrid nano-sensor was found to have a very sensitive response to 6-MP, which was characterized by an enhanced, yellow, emission peak from the FAM. This occurred because a large, conjugated and rigid plane structure formed, which consisted of 6-MP and NCDs–ssDNA. It was found that this structure could be broken up in the presence of specific interactions, which can occur between DNA and Hg2+; during this process, T–Hg2+–T complexes formed. As a result the fluorescence of FAM was quenched. Thus, a rapid, sensitive and selective method for the analysis of 6-MP and Hg2+ was established, and was successfully applied for the analysis of 6-MP and Hg2+ in water samples.

ce:acknowledgment.ce:para: The authors acknowledge the financial support by the Graduate Student Innovation Program (YC2014-S042) and the State Key Laboratory of Food Science and Technology (SKLF-ZZA201302) of Nanchang University.

ce:section.ce:label: Appendix A
ce:section.ce:para: Supplementary data associated with this article can be found in the online version at doi:10.1016/j.bios.2015.06.014
